sanskrit_devanagari:
अव्यक्ताद्व्यक्तयः सर्वाः प्रभवन्त्यहरागमे ।
रात्र्यागमे प्रलीयन्ते तत्रैवाव्यक्तसंज्ञके ॥ १८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
avyaktād vyaktayaḥ sarvāḥ prabhavanty ahar-āgame |
rātry-āgame pralīyante tatraivāvyakta-saṃjñake || 18 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; vedabase renders verse as concatenated text but body matches the canonical reading word-for-word).
exact (Mukundananda house style: h-aspirate marks on śh, single danda visarga; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_08.018 = MBh_06,030.018). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: At the beginning of Brahmā’s day, all living entities become manifest from the unmanifest state, and thereafter, when the night falls, they are merged into the unmanifest again.
BG 8.18: At the advent of Brahma’s day, all living beings emanate from the unmanifest source. And at the fall of his night, all embodied beings again merge into their unmanifest source.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: purport not captured by scraper for this verse

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use, ≤300 chars of full bhāṣya per IP rules)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.18 Ahar-agame, with the coming of day, at the time when Brahma wakes; sarvah vyaktayah, all manifested things — all creatures characterized as moving and non-moving; prabhavanti, emerge, become manifested; avyaktat, from the Unmanifested. At night they merge into that very same Unmanifested.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.18 Thus, at the dawn of a day of Brahma, the manifest entities existing in the three worlds, possessing body, senses, objects, and places of enjoyment appear from the non-manifest (Avyakta), which is the condition of Brahma’s body in that state.

positions.rendering: the same Unmanifested — that unmanifest matrix from which all moving and non-moving things emerge with the coming of day
the non-manifest — which is the condition of Brahmā's body in that state; at night's beginning the manifest entities of the three worlds become non-manifest

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The two readings differ on whether the unmanifest is impersonal cosmic substrate (Shankara) or the latent state of Brahmā's body (Ramanuja); the structural symmetry of the cycle (emergence at day, dissolution at night, same substrate) is identical in both readings. The verse itself is structural and does not adjudicate.

literal_meaning: From the unmanifest, all manifestations spring forth at the coming of day; at the coming of night they merge into that very same called the unmanifest.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The cosmic-day cycle is described from the side of the manifest. At ahar-āgama (day's coming = the start of Brahmā's day), all manifestations (vyaktayaḥ sarvāḥ) emerge from the unmanifest substrate (avyakta). At rātry-āgama (night's coming), they dissolve back into the same unmanifest (tatraiva avyakta-saṃjñake). The traditions agree on the structural cycle. Shankara emphasizes the moving and the non-moving — all creatures characterized as such — emerging and then merging in the unmanifest. Ramanuja reads the unmanifest as the condition of Brahmā's body in that state, with the manifest world (three worlds, body-sense-object-place-of-enjoyment) emerging from it. The verse's emphasis is the symmetric structure: emergence at day, dissolution at night, the same unmanifest substrate persisting beneath.
